questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman who follows a vegetarian diet reports four months of fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance. Her CBC shows Hb 104 g/L with an MCV of 73 fL, and the film is microcytic and hypochromic. Before starting empiric treatment, her physician wants the single most useful test of her body iron stores. Which test should be ordered?
questions.explain: Ferritin is the best single test of iron stores, and a low value is essentially diagnostic of iron deficiency. Remember it is also an acute-phase reactant, so a normal/high value in inflammation does not exclude deficiency — then check transferrin saturation and TIBC.

guide.s: best single test for iron stores

questions.stem: During a sprint, oxygen delivery to a runner's quadriceps rises sharply as the working muscle becomes warmer and more acidic. Which change shifts the oxygen–hemoglobin dissociation curve in the direction responsible for this increased unloading?
questions.explain: Active tissue generates CO₂ and H⁺, which lower hemoglobin's O₂ affinity and shift the curve right (the Bohr effect), unloading more oxygen exactly where metabolism is highest. Cold, low 2,3-BPG, HbF and CO all shift it left.

questions.stem: A trauma patient is hemorrhaging and needs red cells immediately, before his ABO type can be determined. Which red blood cell group can be transfused safely into a recipient of any ABO type?
options: Only a fully cross-matched unit is ever acceptable
questions.explain: Group O red cells lack A and B antigens, making O the universal RBC donor (O-negative is used uncross-matched in emergencies). Group AB, lacking anti-A and anti-B, is the universal RBC recipient.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old presents with pinpoint red spots over both shins, bleeding gums, and heavy menstrual periods, but has never had joint swelling or deep-muscle bleeds. This bleeding pattern points to a problem with which component of hemostasis?
questions.explain: Petechiae, mucosal bleeding (gums, epistaxis) and menorrhagia are the primary-hemostasis / platelet pattern. Deep-tissue bleeds and hemarthroses instead point to a coagulation-factor (secondary hemostasis) problem such as hemophilia.

questions.stem: A term newborn extracts oxygen efficiently from maternal blood across the placenta because fetal hemoglobin has a higher oxygen affinity than adult HbA. Which globin-chain composition characterizes fetal hemoglobin (HbF)?
questions.explain: HbF = 2 alpha + 2 gamma chains. Its higher O₂ affinity (a left-shifted curve) lets the fetus pull oxygen across the placenta; the switch to adult HbA (2 alpha + 2 beta) is why β-globin disorders such as β-thalassemia declare themselves only after birth.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old with months of fatigue has Hb 96 g/L and an MCV of 113 fL. The blood film shows large oval red cells and several neutrophils with six-lobed nuclei. This morphology indicates which underlying process?
questions.explain: Macro-ovalocytes with hypersegmented neutrophils are the hallmark of megaloblastic anemia from impaired DNA synthesis — usually B12 or folate deficiency. The next step is to measure B12 and folate, because B12 deficiency also threatens the nervous system.

questions.stem: A well 24-year-old man has a CBC for an employment medical: Hb 122 g/L, MCV 67 fL, a relatively high RBC count, a normal RDW, and a normal ferritin. The film shows target cells. Which test best confirms the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Marked microcytosis with a preserved Hb, normal RDW, normal ferritin and target cells points to thalassemia trait, not iron deficiency. β-trait is confirmed by an elevated HbA₂ on electrophoresis/HPLC (α-trait may need genetic testing). Do not give iron without proven deficiency.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with confusion and fatigue. She has thrombocytopenia, anemia with schistocytes on the film, a mildly raised creatinine, and a low-grade fever; her PT and PTT are normal. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Reassure and repeat the CBC in a week
questions.explain: Thrombocytopenia with microangiopathic hemolysis (schistocytes) plus neurologic and renal findings and normal coagulation is TTP — a medical emergency treated with plasma exchange (with steroids ± rituximab/caplacizumab). Platelet transfusion is generally avoided unless there is life-threatening bleeding.

questions.stem: An 78-year-old woman with heart failure becomes acutely dyspneic two hours into her second unit of red cells. She is hypertensive with a raised JVP, bibasal crackles, and a markedly elevated BNP, and she improves promptly after furosemide. Which transfusion reaction is this?
questions.explain: Volume overload with hypertension, raised JVP, pulmonary edema, a high BNP, and a diuretic response is TACO. The key contrast is TRALI, which is non-cardiogenic and not driven by volume; risk is reduced by transfusing slowly and one unit at a time in susceptible patients.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man develops sudden hypoxemia and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates within four hours of a plasma transfusion. He is normotensive with a normal JVP and a normal BNP, and an echocardiogram shows good ventricular function. Which reaction is most likely?
questions.explain: Acute hypoxemia with bilateral infiltrates soon after transfusion but without volume overload (normal BP, JVP, BNP and ventricular function) is TRALI — non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from donor antibodies against recipient leukocytes. Management is supportive/respiratory; TACO, by contrast, responds to diuresis.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old with known sickle cell disease is admitted with chest pain, fever, hypoxia, and a new infiltrate on chest radiograph. In addition to analgesia and oxygen, which is a key part of managing this complication?
questions.explain: This is acute chest syndrome, a leading cause of death in sickle cell disease and an emergency: oxygen, antibiotics, analgesia, incentive spirometry, and simple or exchange transfusion in severe or progressive cases.

questions.stem: A pediatrician explains why a 3-year-old with sickle cell disease needs pneumococcal vaccination and daily penicillin prophylaxis. Which feature of the disease underlies this recommendation?
options: Functional asplenia raising the risk of encapsulated-organism infection
questions.explain: Repeated splenic infarction produces functional asplenia, sharply raising the risk of overwhelming infection with encapsulated organisms (pneumococcus, H. influenzae, meningococcus). Hence vaccination and, in young children, penicillin prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old has a megaloblastic anemia, but serum B12 and folate both return borderline-low. You are concerned about missing B12 deficiency, which can cause irreversible neurologic injury. Which test best confirms clinically important B12 deficiency?
questions.explain: Methylmalonic acid is elevated in B12 deficiency but not in folate deficiency (homocysteine rises in both). Confirming B12 deficiency matters because treating with folate alone can correct the anemia while allowing subacute combined degeneration of the cord to progress.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with heavy menstrual bleeding has Hb 96 g/L, MCV 71 fL, a high RDW, a low ferritin, and a platelet count of 585 × 10⁹/L. She has no splenomegaly and feels well apart from fatigue. How should the elevated platelet count be interpreted?
questions.explain: Iron deficiency commonly drives a reactive thrombocytosis; the count typically normalizes once iron is replaced, and no cytoreductive therapy is needed. A persistently high platelet count after iron repletion would prompt reconsideration.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old develops pancytopenia. There is no splenomegaly and no blasts on the film; reticulocytes are low, and a marrow biopsy is markedly hypocellular with fat replacing hematopoietic tissue. Which diagnosis best fits?
questions.explain: A hypocellular ('empty') marrow with pancytopenia, low reticulocytes, and no infiltrating cells or splenomegaly is characteristic of aplastic anemia (marrow failure). Infiltration by leukemia would instead show a hypercellular marrow with blasts.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old has anemia with high reticulocytes, high LDH, high indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin, and numerous spherocytes on the film. Which single test best separates autoimmune hemolytic anemia from hereditary spherocytosis?
questions.explain: Both show spherocytes and a hemolytic picture. A positive direct antiglobulin test indicates antibody-mediated autoimmune hemolysis; hereditary spherocytosis is DAT-negative and is confirmed with tests such as the EMA-binding test or osmotic fragility.

questions.stem: An RhD-negative woman with no anti-D antibodies is pregnant with an RhD-positive fetus. Her obstetrician plans RhD immune globulin. By what mechanism does it prevent hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn in future pregnancies?
options: It passively clears fetal D-positive cells before the mother mounts her own anti-D response
It replaces a missing maternal clotting factor
questions.explain: RhD immune globulin is passive anti-D that binds and clears fetal RhD-positive red cells before the maternal immune system is sensitized, preventing the active anti-D IgG alloimmunization that would cross the placenta and harm a later D-positive fetus.

questions.stem: A woman develops massive postpartum hemorrhage. She is now oozing from every venipuncture site; platelets are 55 × 10⁹/L, PT and PTT are prolonged, fibrinogen is very low, D-dimer is markedly elevated, and schistocytes are present. Beyond urgently controlling the obstetric source, which product most directly corrects the key deficiency driving her bleeding?
options: Prophylactic platelets to keep the count >150
questions.explain: This is DIC with consumptive hypofibrinogenemia. Alongside treating the cause, cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate replaces fibrinogen (with FFP and platelets as guided by labs and bleeding). Definitive control still requires stopping the obstetric hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A man rescued from a house fire is confused and tachypneic. His pulse oximeter reads 98%, yet arterial sampling and his clinical state indicate genuine tissue hypoxia. Which statement best explains the discrepancy?
options: CO binds hemoglobin and shifts the curve right, improving unloading
CO binds hemoglobin, shifts the curve left, and is read as oxyhemoglobin so pulse oximetry is falsely normal
Methemoglobin lowers affinity and unloads more oxygen
Fetal hemoglobin is being produced acutely
questions.explain: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin avidly, forming carboxyhemoglobin that shifts the curve left (impairing unloading) and that a standard pulse oximeter cannot distinguish from oxyhemoglobin — giving a falsely normal SpO₂. Diagnosis needs co-oximetry to measure carboxyhemoglobin; treat with high-flow/100% oxygen.

guide.s: carbon monoxide left shift false oximetry

questions.stem: A previously transfused man is readmitted eight days after an uneventful red-cell transfusion with a falling hemoglobin, mild jaundice, and a newly positive direct antiglobulin test. His original cross-match had been fully compatible. Which transfusion reaction best explains this?
questions.explain: An anamnestic rise in a previously formed but undetectable non-ABO alloantibody (classically Kidd/anti-Jk) days to weeks later causes a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction: a falling Hb, jaundice, and a newly positive DAT despite an initially compatible cross-match. It is usually managed supportively with antigen-negative units thereafter.

cards.prompt: What are the first two questions to ask about any abnormal CBC?
cards.answer: Which cell line(s) are abnormal, and is it one line or several? Multiple lines down points to a marrow (factory) problem until proven otherwise.

cards.text: By MCV, anemia is microcytic if MCV {{<80}} fL, normocytic {{80–100}} fL, and macrocytic if {{>100}} fL.
cards.explain: MCV is the first branch point of the anemia algorithm.

cards.prompt: What two tests form the backbone of the anemia algorithm?
cards.answer: The MCV (cell size) and the reticulocyte count (production versus loss/destruction).

cards.prompt: A high RDW with microcytosis favours which diagnosis over thalassemia trait?
cards.explain: Iron deficiency produces variably sized cells (high RDW); thalassemia trait cells are uniformly small (normal RDW).

cards.prompt: Match the reticulocyte response to its meaning:
pairs: Underproduction: deficiency, renal, inflammation, marrow failure
cards.explain: Reticulocytes separate loss/destruction from a production problem.

cards.prompt: Which physical sign is most directly attributable to anemia itself?
cards.answer: Pallor, especially conjunctival pallor, from reduced hemoglobin and oxygen-carrying capacity.

cards.prompt: Match each red-cell finding to its association:
cards.explain: Film morphology often points straight to the mechanism.

cards.prompt: Which two film findings are 'never-miss', urgent results?
cards.explain: Schistocytes (thrombotic microangiopathy) and blasts (acute leukemia) both demand urgent action.

cards.prompt: Basophilia on the differential should raise suspicion of which group of disorders?
cards.answer: Myeloproliferative neoplasms, especially chronic myeloid leukemia (CML).

cards.text: A low platelet count caused by {{clumping}} in the EDTA tube is called {{pseudothrombocytopenia}} and must be excluded before working up true thrombocytopenia.
cards.explain: Always confirm a low platelet count and review the film for clumps.

cards.prompt: Match each factor to the curve shift it causes:
cards.explain: Right shift unloads O₂ to active tissue; left shift holds onto it.

cards.text: Adult HbA is made of 2 {{alpha}} and 2 {{beta}} chains, each with a heme, so one hemoglobin molecule binds up to {{4}} oxygen molecules.
cards.explain: Four hemes per tetramer; binding is cooperative (sigmoid curve).

cards.prompt: What is the Bohr effect?
cards.answer: In active tissue, rising CO₂ and H⁺ lower hemoglobin's O₂ affinity (a right shift), promoting oxygen unloading where it is needed.

cards.prompt: Carbon monoxide poisoning classically produces which oxygen-delivery picture?
options: Right shift with a low SpO₂
Left shift with a falsely normal SpO₂
cards.explain: CO shifts the curve left and is read as oxyhemoglobin by standard pulse oximetry, giving a falsely normal SpO₂; use co-oximetry.

cards.prompt: Match the life stage to its main hematopoietic site:
cards.explain: Hematopoiesis migrates from yolk sac to liver to axial marrow.

cards.prompt: What does pancytopenia usually imply about the cause?
cards.answer: All three lines down points to a bone-marrow (factory) problem — failure, infiltration/replacement, or ineffective hematopoiesis — until proven otherwise.

cards.prompt: Match the pancytopenia mechanism to its example:
cards.explain: Categorize, then test for reversible causes and consider a marrow biopsy.

cards.prompt: Which findings in pancytopenia are urgent red flags? (select all)
cards.explain: Febrile neutropenia, very low platelets/bleeding and blasts all demand urgent action.

cards.prompt: Match each iron study to its value in iron deficiency:
cards.explain: Low ferritin + low saturation + high TIBC is the classic iron-deficiency pattern.

cards.text: Low ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but ferritin is an {{acute-phase reactant}}, so a {{normal/high}} value does not exclude deficiency during inflammation.
cards.explain: In inflammation, confirm with transferrin saturation/TIBC.

cards.prompt: A man or postmenopausal woman has new iron-deficiency anemia. What must be excluded?
cards.answer: GI blood loss, including malignancy, until proven otherwise — evaluate the GI tract.

cards.prompt: Which is a clear indication for IV rather than oral iron?
cards.explain: IV iron is used for intolerance, malabsorption, ongoing loss beyond oral replacement, or when rapid repletion is needed.

cards.text: A craving for ice is called {{pagophagia}} and is a classic symptom of {{iron deficiency}}.
cards.explain: Pica/pagophagia, restless legs and koilonychia are iron-deficiency clues.

cards.prompt: Why must you avoid empiric iron in microcytosis due to thalassemia?
cards.answer: Thalassemia microcytosis is from reduced globin production, not iron lack; giving iron without proven deficiency risks iron overload.

cards.text: β-thalassemia trait typically shows a very low MCV with a {{normal}} RDW and an elevated {{HbA₂}} on electrophoresis.
cards.explain: Contrast with iron deficiency: high RDW, low ferritin, normal electrophoresis.

cards.prompt: Match each sickle cell complication to a key point:
pairs: Prompt analgesia, hydration, O₂ only if hypoxic
cards.explain: Vaso-occlusion and hemolysis drive the clinical picture.

cards.prompt: How does hydroxyurea modify sickle cell disease, and what must be monitored?
cards.answer: It increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF), reducing vaso-occlusive episodes; monitor the CBC for myelosuppression.

cards.prompt: Which set of labs indicates active hemolysis?
options: High reticulocytes, high LDH, high indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin
Low reticulocytes, low LDH, high haptoglobin
Low retics, high ferritin, normal LDH
cards.explain: Hemolysis: reticulocytosis with high LDH and indirect bilirubin and low haptoglobin.

cards.prompt: Match the cause of thrombocytopenia to its clue:
pairs: Isolated low platelets, otherwise normal CBC/film
Low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, prolonged PT/PTT
cards.explain: Coagulation studies and the film separate these quickly.

cards.prompt: Order these platelet counts from least to most dangerous (lowest bleeding risk first):
cards.explain: Risk rises as the count falls; <10 carries serious spontaneous bleeding risk.

cards.prompt: Which is true of platelet transfusion in TTP?
cards.explain: In TTP, plasma exchange is the treatment; platelet transfusion is generally avoided unless there is life-threatening bleeding.

cards.prompt: How is immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) managed across severities?
cards.answer: Observe mild, isolated cases; use steroids or IVIG for severe bleeding or a very low count.

cards.text: Thrombocytopenia plus {{schistocytes}} is an emergency until {{TTP/DIC/HUS}} is excluded.
cards.explain: Microangiopathy with low platelets is a never-miss combination.

cards.prompt: Match each ABO group to its plasma antibodies:
cards.explain: Antibodies are directed against the antigens the person lacks.

cards.prompt: What is the mechanism of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction from ABO mismatch?
cards.answer: Recipient naturally occurring IgM binds donor RBC ABO antigens, activates complement, and causes intravascular hemolysis.

cards.prompt: Order the immediate steps for a suspected acute hemolytic transfusion reaction:
items: Maintain IV access with normal saline
Notify the blood bank; send DAT + hemolysis samples
Support BP and renal perfusion; manage DIC
cards.explain: Stop first, then resuscitate, verify, notify, and support organs.

cards.prompt: Match each transfusion reaction to its feature:
pairs: Volume overload: hypertension, raised JVP, responds to diuretic
Days-weeks later, anamnestic alloantibody, positive DAT
cards.explain: TACO is overload; TRALI is non-cardiogenic; ABO is the catastrophic immediate one.

cards.text: Anti-D antibodies are {{not}} naturally occurring; they form after exposure to D-positive cells through {{transfusion or pregnancy}}, and are prevented by {{RhD immune globulin}}.
cards.explain: Contrast with ABO antibodies, which are naturally occurring.

cards.prompt: Severe allergic/anaphylactic transfusion reactions are classically associated with which recipient factor?
cards.explain: IgA-deficient recipients can react to IgA in donor plasma, risking anaphylaxis.

cards.prompt: Who is the universal RBC donor and the universal RBC recipient?
cards.answer: Group O is the universal RBC donor (no A/B antigens); group AB is the universal RBC recipient (no anti-A/anti-B antibodies).

cards.text: In sickle cell disease, {{deoxygenated}} HbS {{polymerizes}}, causing sickling and leading to {{hemolysis}} and {{vaso-occlusion}}.
cards.explain: Polymerization on deoxygenation drives both hallmark processes.

cards.prompt: Match each white cell to its core function:
cards.explain: The differential localizes the type of immune problem.

cards.prompt: Order the initial workup of a microcytic anemia:
items: Ferritin + iron studies (saturation/TIBC) and review the film
Hemoglobin electrophoresis if iron studies are normal
GI/gynecologic evaluation for the source of blood loss
cards.explain: Confirm iron status first; pursue thalassemia and the bleeding source next.

cards.prompt: Which labs fit iron deficiency anemia? (select all)
cards.explain: Iron deficiency lowers ferritin and transferrin saturation but raises TIBC and RDW.

cards.prompt: How is TACO (circulatory overload) managed and prevented?
cards.answer: Treat as volume overload — slow or divide transfusions, diurese, sit the patient up, and give oxygen as needed.

cards.prompt: When is a bone marrow biopsy especially indicated in pancytopenia?
cards.answer: When it is unexplained, severe, persistent, or associated with abnormal cells such as blasts on the film.

cards.prompt: Why is 'anemia' never an acceptable final diagnosis?
cards.answer: It is only a clue to an underlying process (loss, destruction, or underproduction); you must identify the cause.

guides.title: Week 14 · 1 CBC & the Anemia Approach
guides.html:
Foundations

CBC & the Anemia Approach

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

How to read any abnormal CBC: which line(s), then MCV and reticulocytes for anemia. Anemia is a clue, never a final diagnosis.

The five questions for any abnormal CBC

  • Which cell line? Red (anemia/erythrocytosis), white (leukopenia/leukocytosis), platelets (low/high).
  • One line or many? A single line down → missing raw material, loss/destruction, or a lineage problem. Bicytopenia/pancytopenia → a bone-marrow (factory) problem until proven otherwise.
  • For anemia, what is the MCV? Microcytic <80, normocytic 80–100, macrocytic >100 fL.
  • What is the reticulocyte response? High → marrow responding (bleeding/hemolysis). Low/inappropriately normal → an underproduction problem.
  • What does the film show? Often the single most useful next test in an unexplained CBC.

You have not truly reviewed a CBC until you have looked at Hb / MCV / RDW, the WBC and differential (including the absolute neutrophil count), and the platelet count.

MCV buckets

MCVCommon causes
Microcytic <80Iron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of inflammation, sideroblastic, lead
Normocytic 80–100Acute blood loss, hemolysis, renal disease, inflammation, marrow failure, early iron deficiency
Macrocytic >100B12/folate deficiency, alcohol, liver disease, hypothyroidism, drugs, MDS, reticulocytosis

RDW and the reticulocyte count

RDW measures variability of red-cell size. A high RDW with microcytosis favours iron deficiency (a mix of old normal and new small cells); a normal RDW with marked microcytosis favours thalassemia trait (uniformly small cells).

The reticulocyte count separates loss/destruction from underproduction. A high count after a few days means the marrow is responding (bleeding or hemolysis); a low or inappropriately normal count means a production problem — nutrient deficiency, renal disease (low EPO), inflammation, or marrow failure.

MCV + reticulocyte count is the backbone of the anemia algorithm. Classify by size, then ask whether the marrow is responding.

Worked approach

StepWhat you do
1Confirm anemia; assess severity and stability (bleeding? tissue hypoxia?)
2Isolated, or with other cytopenias? (the latter suggests marrow/systemic cause)
3Classify by MCV; order the targeted first tests for that bucket
4Use reticulocytes ± the film to refine and to decide on marrow study
Anemia: symptoms & signs
Fatigue, exertional dyspnea, lightheadedness, headache, conjunctival pallor, tachycardia; chest pain/ischemia, heart failure, or hypotension in severe or rapid anemia. Severity depends on speed as much as on the number.
Anemia is never the final diagnosis — always identify the cause.

guides.title: Week 14 · 2 The Blood Film
guides.html:
Morphology

The Blood Film

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Pattern recognition on the smear — the red-cell, white-cell, and platelet findings that point straight to a diagnosis.

Red-cell morphology that changes management

FindingThink
Microcytic / hypochromicIron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of inflammation, sideroblastic, lead
Macro-ovalocytes + hypersegmented neutrophilsMegaloblastic anemia (B12 or folate)
Schistocytes (fragments)Microangiopathy — TTP, HUS, DIC, mechanical valve (urgent)
Spherocytes (no central pallor)Hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis
Target cellsThalassemia, liver disease, post-splenectomy
Sickle cellsSickle cell disease
Teardrop cells + leukoerythroblastic filmMarrow infiltration / fibrosis
Bite cells / Heinz bodiesOxidative hemolysis (e.g. G6PD deficiency)
Basophilic stipplingLead poisoning, sideroblastic anemia, thalassemia
Howell–Jolly bodiesHyposplenism / post-splenectomy
PolychromasiaReticulocytosis (marrow responding)
BlastsAcute leukemia (urgent)

White cells

CellRole & clinical relevance
NeutrophilsBacterial/fungal defense; severe neutropenia is a febrile emergency
LymphocytesAdaptive immunity (T, B, NK); lymphocytosis may be reactive or malignant
MonocytesPhagocytosis, antigen presentation; monocytosis in chronic inflammation or myeloid neoplasms
EosinophilsAllergy, parasites, drug reactions, some malignancies
BasophilsBasophilia → myeloproliferative disease, especially CML

Platelets on the film

Giant platelets suggest increased turnover or an inherited platelet disorder. Platelet clumping in the EDTA tube causes spurious (pseudo-) thrombocytopenia — always exclude it before a work-up by checking the film and, if needed, repeating in a citrate tube.

Two film findings you never miss
Schistocytes (thrombotic microangiopathy — TTP/HUS/DIC) and blasts (acute leukemia). Either one changes the pace and the plan immediately.
The film is the cheapest high-yield test in hematology — review it whenever the CBC is unexplained, severe, or clinically concerning.

guides.title: Week 14 · 3 Oxygen Delivery & Hemoglobin
guides.html:
Physiology

Oxygen Delivery & Hemoglobin

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

How hemoglobin carries and releases oxygen — cooperative binding, the sigmoid curve, and what shifts it.

Hemoglobin structure

Most oxygen travels bound to hemoglobin inside red cells; only a small fraction is dissolved in plasma. Adult HbA = 2α + 2β chains, each of the four globin chains carrying a heme with iron that binds one O₂ — so one Hb molecule binds up to 4 O₂. Fetal HbF = 2α + 2γ, with higher O₂ affinity so the fetus extracts oxygen across the placenta.

Cooperative binding & the curve

Binding of one O₂ raises hemoglobin's affinity for the next, producing the classic sigmoidal dissociation curve. The P₅₀ (the PO₂ at which Hb is 50% saturated) summarizes affinity: a higher P₅₀ = right shift (lower affinity), a lower P₅₀ = left shift.

ShiftAffinity / effectCauses
RightLower affinity → more unloading to tissue↑CO₂, ↑H⁺/low pH, ↑temperature, ↑2,3-BPG, exercise
LeftHigher affinity → less unloading↓CO₂, alkalosis, ↓temperature, ↓2,3-BPG, HbF, carbon monoxide
Bohr effect
Metabolically active tissue generates CO₂ and H⁺, which lower Hb affinity, shift the curve right, and promote O₂ unloading exactly where it is needed. In the lungs the reverse occurs, favouring loading.

Special cases

2,3-BPG rises in chronic hypoxia/anemia and at altitude (right shift, better unloading); stored (banked) blood is depleted of 2,3-BPG, transiently shifting it left. Methemoglobin (iron oxidized to Fe³⁺) cannot carry O₂ and shifts the residual curve left.

Carbon monoxide
CO binds hemoglobin with ~250× the affinity of O₂, shifts the curve left, and is read as oxyhemoglobin by standard pulse oximetry — giving a falsely normal SpO₂ despite real tissue hypoxia. Suspect it clinically and measure carboxyhemoglobin on co-oximetry.

guides.title: Week 14 · 4 Hematopoiesis & Pancytopenia
guides.html:
Marrow

Hematopoiesis & Pancytopenia

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Where blood is made, how production is regulated, and how to think about the patient whose marrow factory is failing.

Hematopoiesis

Mature blood cells arise from hematopoietic stem cells in marrow through four properties: self-renewal (maintain the pool), commitment (choose a lineage), proliferation (expand progenitors), and maturation (become functional cells). Production is tuned by growth factors — erythropoietin (EPO) from the kidney drives red cells, thrombopoietin (TPO) drives platelets, and G-CSF drives neutrophils.

StageMain site
Early embryoYolk sac
FetusFetal liver, then marrow later in gestation
ChildhoodThroughout much of the skeleton
AdultAxial skeleton: pelvis, vertebrae, ribs, sternum, proximal long bones

Pancytopenia = all three lines down

Anemia + leukopenia/neutropenia + thrombocytopenia. It usually signals a marrow problem — failure, replacement, or ineffective hematopoiesis — until proven otherwise. (Two lines down is bicytopenia and carries the same worry.)

CategoryExampleClue
Marrow failureAplastic anemiaHypocellular ("empty") marrow, no splenomegaly
Infiltration / replacementLeukemia, lymphoma, metastasis, myelofibrosisBlasts, teardrops, leukoerythroblastic film
Ineffective hematopoiesisB12/folate deficiency, MDSMacrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils
Destruction / sequestrationHypersplenism, autoimmune cytopeniasSplenomegaly
Infection / drugs / toxinsHIV, EBV, hepatitis, chemotherapy, alcoholExposure history

Work-up

  • Confirm true pancytopenia on a repeat CBC; review the differential and absolute neutrophil count.
  • Review the blood film urgently; check the reticulocyte count.
  • Screen reversible causes: B12/folate, iron studies, liver and renal tests, viral testing; hemolysis labs if reticulocytes are high.
  • Bone marrow aspirate/biopsy when unexplained, severe, persistent, or associated with abnormal cells.
Urgent red flags
Febrile neutropenia · platelets <10 or active bleeding · blasts on the smear · severe symptomatic anemia · rapidly falling counts · schistocytes/neuro/renal failure (TTP/HUS).

guides.title: Week 14 · 5 Iron Deficiency Anemia
guides.html:
Anemia

Iron Deficiency Anemia

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The most common anemia worldwide: the stages, the lab pattern, the ferritin trap, treatment, and finding the cause.

Stages & lab pattern

Iron deficiency progresses: depleted stores (low ferritin, normal CBC) → iron-restricted erythropoiesis (low transferrin saturation, high TIBC, MCV starting to fall) → iron deficiency anemia (low Hb, low MCV, high RDW, microcytic hypochromic film).

TestIron deficiency
FerritinLow (best single test of stores)
Serum iron / transferrin saturationLow
TIBC / transferrinHigh
RDWHigh
PlateletsNormal or reactive thrombocytosis
The ferritin trap
Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant. A low ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but a normal or high ferritin does not exclude it in inflammation, infection, or malignancy — interpret with transferrin saturation/TIBC and the clinical picture.

Iron deficiency vs thalassemia trait

Both give microcytosis; the distinction matters because giving iron in thalassemia is useless and risks overload.

FeatureIron deficiencyThalassemia trait
RDWHighNormal / mildly elevated
RBC countLow/normalNormal/high relative to Hb
FerritinLowNormal/high
ElectrophoresisNormalβ-trait: elevated HbA₂

Symptoms, treatment & cause

Beyond fatigue and pallor, look for pica/pagophagia (ice craving), restless legs, brittle nails/koilonychia, and glossitis/angular cheilitis. Management has two goals: replace iron and treat the cause.

  • Oral iron (ferrous sulfate/fumarate/gluconate), often once daily or alternate-day to improve absorption and tolerability; take away from calcium, antacids, tea/coffee; continue after the Hb normalizes to refill stores. Expect a reticulocyte bump within a week and Hb rise over weeks.
  • IV iron when oral is not tolerated, malabsorption is present, blood loss outpaces oral replacement, rapid repletion is needed, or chronic inflammation limits oral efficacy.
  • Transfusion only for instability, active major bleeding, or tissue hypoxia — it raises oxygen-carrying capacity temporarily but does not correct the deficiency.
New iron deficiency in a man or postmenopausal woman warrants evaluation for GI blood loss / malignancy until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 14 · 6 Thalassemia & Sickle Cell
guides.html:
Hemoglobinopathies

Thalassemia & Sickle Cell

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Two inherited routes to anemia: too little globin (thalassemia) and abnormal globin (sickle). Both selected for by malaria.

Thalassemia — reduced globin production

Reduced α or β chain synthesis leaves an imbalance that damages precursors and circulating cells → microcytosis ± hemolysis. Because α chains are needed for both fetal and adult Hb, severe α-thalassemia presents early; β-thalassemia declares itself after the fetal-to-adult Hb switch.

TypeKey features
Silent carrier / traitAsymptomatic or mild microcytosis; β-trait shows ↑HbA₂
IntermediaVariable anemia, splenomegaly, iron-overload risk
MajorSevere transfusion-dependent anemia, marrow expansion, skeletal change, growth failure
Do not give iron blindly
Microcytosis is not always iron deficiency. In thalassemia, do not prescribe iron unless deficiency is confirmed — unnecessary iron risks overload. Offer genetic/family-planning counselling where relevant.

Sickle cell disease

A structural β-globin point mutation makes HbS; on deoxygenation HbS polymerizes, sickling the cell → chronic hemolysis + vaso-occlusion. Crises are triggered by infection, hypoxia, dehydration, cold, and stress.

ComplicationNote
Vaso-occlusive crisisPrompt analgesia (often opioids), hydration, treat triggers, O₂ only if hypoxic
Acute chest syndromeChest pain + fever + hypoxia + new infiltrate; O₂, antibiotics, analgesia, ± (exchange) transfusion (emergency)
Functional aspleniaRepeated infarction → encapsulated-organism risk → vaccinate + penicillin prophylaxis in children
OthersStroke, splenic sequestration, dactylitis, priapism, avascular necrosis, CKD, pigment gallstones

Diagnosis is by newborn screening and hemoglobin electrophoresis/HPLC; hemolysis labs show high reticulocytes, high LDH and indirect bilirubin, and low haptoglobin.

Disease-modifying therapy
Hydroxyurea raises HbF and reduces vaso-occlusive episodes (monitor the CBC for myelosuppression). Folic acid supports the increased erythropoiesis; vaccination and, in selected patients, hematopoietic stem-cell transplantation (curative) round out care.

guides.title: Week 14 · 7 Thrombocytopenia & Bleeding
guides.html:
Hemostasis

Thrombocytopenia & Bleeding

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Reading the bleeding pattern, the causes that matter, and the counts and film findings that signal an emergency.

Bleeding pattern localizes the defect

Platelet / primary hemostasisCoagulation factor / secondary
Petechiae, purpuraDeep tissue bleeds
Mucosal bleeding, epistaxis, gum bleedingHemarthroses, large hematomas
Menorrhagia; immediate bleeding after cutsDelayed bleeding after procedures

Causes that change management

CauseCluePrinciple
ITPIsolated thrombocytopenia, otherwise normal CBC/filmSteroids/IVIG if severe bleeding or very low count; observe mild cases
TTPThrombocytopenia + schistocytes + neuro/renal ± fever, normal coagulationEmergency plasma exchange; avoid platelet transfusion
DICSepsis/trauma/obstetric; ↑PT/PTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↑D-dimer, schistocytesTreat the cause; product support (incl. cryoprecipitate)
Marrow failure / leukemiaOther cytopenias, blastsUrgent hematology + marrow study
Drug-induced / hypersplenismNew drug; splenomegaly/liver diseaseStop culprit; treat underlying
Distinguishing the microangiopathies by labs
ITP: coagulation normal, no schistocytes. TTP: coagulation normal, schistocytes present. DIC: prolonged PT/PTT, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, schistocytes. In pregnancy also think gestational thrombocytopenia and HELLP.

Platelet thresholds

  • <50 ×10⁹/L — increased bleeding risk with procedures/trauma
  • <20 ×10⁹/L — higher spontaneous bleeding risk
  • <10 ×10⁹/L — significant risk of spontaneous serious bleeding, including intracranial
Film red flags & first step
Always exclude pseudothrombocytopenia from clumping first. Then: schistocytes → TTP/HUS/DIC; blasts → acute leukemia. Both are urgent.

guides.title: Week 14 · 8 Blood Groups & Transfusion
guides.html:
Transfusion

Blood Groups & Transfusion

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

ABO and Rh, the catastrophic ABO mismatch, the named transfusion reactions, and preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn.

ABO system

ABO antigens are red-cell surface carbohydrates; the matching antibodies are naturally occurring IgM against whichever antigen the person lacks.

GroupRBC antigensPlasma antibodiesCan receive RBCs from
AAAnti-BA, O
BBAnti-AB, O
ABA + BNoneA, B, AB, O (universal RBC recipient)
ONoneAnti-A + Anti-BO only (universal RBC donor)

Before a planned transfusion the lab does a type and screen (ABO/Rh + antibody screen) and a cross-match; in extreme emergencies, O-negative RBCs are given uncross-matched.

Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (ABO mismatch)
Usually a clerical error. Recipient IgM binds donor antigens → complement → intravascular hemolysis: fever, flank/back pain, hypotension, hemoglobinuria, DIC, AKI. Stop the transfusion, give saline, do a clerical check, notify the blood bank, send a DAT and hemolysis labs, and support blood pressure and the kidneys.

Named reactions

ReactionFeatures
Febrile non-hemolyticFever/chills, no hemolysis (cytokines); common and benign
Allergic / anaphylaxisUrticaria → anaphylaxis; severe reactions in IgA deficiency
TACOCirculatory overload: dyspnea, hypertension, raised JVP, pulmonary edema, ↑BNP; responds to diuretic
TRALIAcute hypoxemia + bilateral infiltrates within 6 h, non-cardiogenic, normal volume status
Delayed hemolyticDays–weeks later; anamnestic alloantibody (e.g. Kidd); falling Hb, positive DAT

Rh & hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn

Anti-D is not naturally occurring — it forms only after exposure to D-positive RBCs (transfusion or pregnancy). An RhD-negative mother sensitized to a D-positive fetus makes anti-D IgG that crosses the placenta in a later D-positive pregnancy → fetal hemolysis. Alloimmunization to non-ABO antigens similarly threatens future pregnancies and transfusions.

Prevention
RhD immune globulin (passive anti-D) given to appropriate RhD-negative patients clears fetal D-positive cells before the maternal immune system responds, preventing anti-D alloimmunization.

guides.title: Week 14 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Blood I

Week 14 · Blood I
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Severe symptomatic anemia (chest pain, syncope, heart failure) → transfuse
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria) → STOP the transfusion + supportive care
TACO vs TRALI (dyspnea during/after transfusion) → stop, support; TACO = overload (diuresis), TRALI = within 6 h
Pancytopenia + blasts → acute leukemia until proven otherwise — urgent heme + marrow
Platelets <10 or wet purpura (oral blood blisters) → serious spontaneous-bleeding risk
Acute chest syndrome (sickle cell) → O₂, antibiotics, transfusion / exchange
TTP (schistocytes + low platelets, normal coags) → plasma exchange; no platelets

CBC & anemia approach Approach

MCV micro <80 · normo 80–100 · macro >100
Reticulocytes high = bleeding/hemolysis · low = underproduction
RDW high + micro → iron deficiency (thalassemia = normal RDW)
Microcytic iron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease, sideroblastic
Macrocytic B12/folate, alcohol, liver, hypothyroid, MDS, drugs
First questions which line(s)? one or several?

Blood film Smear

Schistocytes MAHA / DIC / TTP / mechanical valve
Blasts acute leukemia
Target cells thalassemia, liver disease, post-splenectomy
Spherocytes hereditary spherocytosis / AIHA
Teardrops + leukoerythroblastic myelofibrosis / marrow infiltration
Bite cells / Heinz bodies G6PD deficiency (oxidative)

Oxygen delivery & Hb Physiology

O₂ delivery cardiac output × Hb × SaO₂
Left shift (↑affinity) ↓2,3-BPG, CO, alkalosis, low temp, HbF
Right shift (↓affinity) ↑2,3-BPG, acidosis, fever, exercise
Anemia symptoms fatigue, dyspnea, pallor; demand ischemia in CAD
CO poisoning normal PaO₂/SpO₂, falsely reassuring; check co-oximetry

Hematopoiesis & pancytopenia Marrow

Pancytopenia marrow (aplastic/leukemia/MDS/infiltration), B12/folate, hypersplenism
Red flags febrile neutropenia, blasts, platelets <10
Multiple lines down factory (marrow) problem until proven otherwise
Workup film, reticulocytes, B12/folate; marrow if unexplained
ESA erythropoiesis-stimulating agent for anemia of CKD

Iron deficiency Iron

Best single test ferritin (low = deficient)
Caveat ferritin is an acute-phase reactant → check transferrin sat / TIBC
Film microcytic, hypochromic, high RDW
Iron studies ID: ↓ferritin, ↑TIBC, ↓sat; ACD: ↓/normal, ↓TIBC
Adults find the source (GI bleed) — scope if indicated
Treat oral iron; IV iron if intolerant / malabsorption / CKD

Thalassemia & sickle cell Hemoglobinopathy

Thalassemia trait micro, normal RDW, normal/high RBC count; Hb electrophoresis
Don't give iron for thalassemia trait
Sickle cell vaso-occlusive crises; pain, dactylitis
Acute chest syndrome O₂, antibiotics, transfusion/exchange
Asplenia (autosplenectomy) encapsulated-organism risk; vaccinate
Hydroxyurea ↑HbF → fewer crises

Thrombocytopenia & bleeding Platelets

ITP isolated thrombocytopenia, normal film
TTP schistocytes + neuro/renal → plasma exchange (no platelets)
DIC ↑PT/PTT, ↓fibrinogen, schistocytes
HIT platelets ↓>50%, day 5–10 + thrombosis
Wet purpura oral blood blisters = high serious-bleeding risk
Thresholds <50 procedure · <20 spontaneous · <10 serious

Blood groups & transfusion Transfusion

Pre-transfusion ABO/Rh type & screen / crossmatch
Acute hemolytic ABO mismatch → stop, support (intravascular)
Febrile non-hemolytic / allergic common, usually mild
TACO vs TRALI overload (diurese) vs acute lung injury (<6 h)
Delayed hemolytic anamnestic antibody, days later
Massive transfusion citrate → ↓Ca; coagulopathy, ↑/↓K
Week 14 · Blood I · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.mechanism: Replaces iron stores for hemoglobin synthesis
facets.monitoring: Best absorbed on empty stomach / with vitamin C; recheck Hb + ferritin

facets.use: Iron deficiency when oral iron fails / malabsorption / CKD

facets.monitoring: Treat B12 before/with folate to avoid masking + worsening neuro deficits

facets.use: Sickle cell disease (reduce crises / acute chest syndrome)
